title: Usernames Are Coming to WhatsApp Soon. Here's How to Reserve Yours
gist: WhatsApp, used by over 3 billion people, is rolling out usernames as a privacy-preserving alternative to phone-number-based contact, explicitly modeling the feature on Signal's 2024 username launch, with celebrity and look-alike names held back for legitimate owners.

insights.claim: WhatsApp, used by over 3 billion people, is rolling usernames out of beta later this year as an additional, privacy-friendlier way to connect without sharing phone numbers, with reservations opening this week.
insights.speaker: Reece Rogers
insights.so_what: A feature change at 3-billion-user scale is a meaningful platform shift; the reservation window creates a short-term user-acquisition/engagement moment analogous to early domain-name or handle land-grabs.
insights.quote: Later this year, the messaging app used by over 3 billion people plans to add usernames.

insights.claim: Users can port their existing Instagram or Facebook account name to WhatsApp during the reservation period, giving Meta's cross-app identity graph a built-in advantage in username claiming.
insights.speaker: Reece Rogers
insights.so_what: Cross-app identity portability within Meta's ecosystem reinforces platform lock-in and gives existing Meta-app users first-mover advantage over new WhatsApp-only users.
insights.quote: you'll have the option to create a new username or port over your existing name from Instagram or Facebook

insights.claim: Users can add an extra security layer by requiring a unique four-digit key in addition to their username before allowing new contacts to reach them.
insights.speaker: Reece Rogers
insights.so_what: A concrete anti-spam/anti-harassment mechanism baked into the username system, relevant for evaluating WhatsApp's approach to abuse mitigation versus competitors.
insights.quote: Users can also add an extra layer of security by only allowing people who know a unique four-digit key, in addition to their username, to contact them.

insights.claim: WhatsApp VP of Product Alice Newton-Rex explicitly compares the new feature to Signal, which rolled out usernames in 2024, saying WhatsApp's system 'will work in a very similar way.'
insights.speaker: Reece Rogers
insights.so_what: WhatsApp is directly following a feature precedent set by privacy-focused Signal roughly two years earlier, indicating privacy-messaging features are diffusing from niche apps into mainstream mass-market platforms.
insights.quote: Signal usernames are probably a good comparison. This will work in a very similar way.

insights.claim: There is no public list of WhatsApp usernames for people to search through, distinguishing it from more discoverable username/handle systems on social platforms.
insights.speaker: Reece Rogers
insights.so_what: The deliberate non-searchability design choice signals WhatsApp is optimizing for private 1:1 contact-sharing rather than public discoverability/social-graph growth, unlike Instagram-style handles.
insights.quote: There's no public list of usernames for people to search through.

insights.claim: Other messaging apps are experimenting with alternative connection methods without sharing numbers, such as Germ DM's 'burner cards' letting users create different connection identities for different groups.
insights.speaker: Reece Rogers
insights.so_what: Signals broader industry experimentation around phone-number-decoupled identity in messaging, a trend area beyond just WhatsApp and Signal.
insights.quote: Germ DM allows its users to create "burner cards" so people can connect with multiple groups in different ways.

insights.claim: High-profile names belonging to public figures, celebrities, and government entities are held back and can only ever be claimed by their legitimate owners, and look-alike derivative spellings of known names are also blocked.
insights.speaker: Reece Rogers
insights.so_what: Proactive celebrity-name protection at launch avoids the impersonation/squatting problems that plagued early Twitter and other platforms before verification systems existed.
insights.quote: The highest-profile names, like public figures or government entities or celebrities, are held. So, they can only ever be claimed by their legitimate owners. Look-alike derivatives of known names are also held back as well.

insights.claim: Newton-Rex expects strong adoption of usernames but says actual uptake will only be confirmed as WhatsApp begins rolling the feature out.
insights.speaker: Reece Rogers
insights.so_what: An executive's own hedged, low-conviction adoption forecast — worth revisiting once actual usage data becomes available post-launch.
insights.quote: I do think that we'll see a lot of adoption, but that's going to be one of the things that we learn as we start rolling it out
